flashcards.front: What is a process in an operating system?
flashcards.back: A process is a program in execution. It is a dynamic entity that represents a program during its execution, containing program code, current activity, and associated resources like memory, CPU registers, and I/O status.

flashcards.front: What is a Process Control Block (PCB)?
flashcards.back: PCB is a data structure that stores information about a process. It contains process ID, process state, program counter, CPU registers, memory management info, I/O status, and accounting information.
The New state is when a process is being created. The OS is loading the program into memory and initializing the PCB. The process has not yet entered the ready queue.
A process in Ready state is waiting in the ready queue to get CPU time. It has all necessary resources except the CPU. The OS scheduler will dispatch it when CPU becomes available.

Running state means the process is currently being executed by the CPU. The process has control of the processor and its instructions are being executed. Only one process can run per CPU core at a time.

Blocked state occurs when a process cannot continue execution until some event occurs, such as I/O completion, signal reception, or availability of a resource. It is not eligible for CPU time until the event completes.
Terminated state is when a process has finished execution or has been explicitly killed. The OS deallocates its resources but may retain its PCB temporarily for accounting purposes.
Dispatch is the event of transferring the CPU control from the OS to a process selected from the ready queue. It moves a process from Ready state to Running state.
Preemption occurs when the OS forcibly removes a running process from the CPU and returns it to the ready queue, typically due to time slice expiration or a higher-priority process becoming ready.
Context switching is the process of saving the state (registers, program counter, etc.) of a currently running process and restoring the state of another process to resume execution.

flashcards.front: What event causes transition from Running to Ready state?
flashcards.back: Preemption (time slice expiration) or when a higher-priority process becomes ready causes the OS to preempt the running process and move it back to the ready queue.

flashcards.front: What event causes transition from Running to Blocked state?
flashcards.back: An I/O request, waiting for a resource, waiting for a child process, or receiving a signal causes the running process to block itself and enter the blocked/waiting state.

flashcards.front: What event causes transition from Blocked to Ready state?
flashcards.back: Completion of the I/O operation, arrival of the requested resource, or occurrence of the awaited event moves the process from blocked state back to the ready queue.
A zombie process is a terminated process whose parent has not yet read its exit status. The process entry remains in the process table until the parent reads the status via wait() system call.
An orphan process is a running child process whose parent has terminated or crashed. Orphan processes are adopted by the init process (PID 1) which performs wait() for them.
